title: Deploy to Cloudflare
description: Publish your Capsa docs to Cloudflare Pages — free, global, and fast.
excerpt: Capsa builds to a static site, so it runs on Cloudflare Pages and the global edge for free. Two ways: the dashboard (Git integration) or the Wrangler CLI.
body: deploy to cloudflare pages capsa builds to a static site, so it runs on cloudflare pages and the global edge for free. two ways: the dashboard (git integration) or the wrangler cli. prerequisites a cloudflare account (the free plan is enough). your docs pushed to github or gitlab. the repo pins pnpm via the root field, so cloudflare uses the right pnpm version automatically. option a — git integration (recommended) 1. push your docs repo to github/gitlab. 2. in the dashboard: workers & pages → create → pages → connect to git, and pick your repo. 3. set the build configuration: setting | value | ---------|-------| framework preset | none | root directory | (repo root) | build command | | build output directory | | 4. under settings → variables and secrets, add a build variable to pin node, plus any site config: variable | example | ----------|---------| | | | | | | 5. save and deploy. every push to your production branch redeploys; every other branch and pr gets a preview url. :::tip set to your final domain so and get absolute urls. ::: spa routing capsa is a single-page app, so deep links like must serve and let the client router take over. this repo ships : real files ( , , ) still take precedence — only unmatched paths fall through to the app. without this file, direct loads and refreshes of doc urls return 404. environment variables are build-time vite inlines variables at build time, so set them as pages build variables before the build — changing one requires a redeploy. :::warning values are compiled into the public bundle. never put a secret (api key, token) in one. the included workos auth uses a public client id, which is fine; anything truly secret belongs in a server/function, not a var. ::: production and preview environments can hold different values — e.g. point preview deploys at a staging . option b — wrangler (cli) set variables in the dashboard, or per deploy with : custom domain in your pages project: custom domains → set up a domain, point dns at cloudflare, and you're live on https in a minute or two. troubleshooting symptom | fix | ---------|-----| doc urls 404 on refresh / direct load | ensure exists (see above). | build fails: | keep the field in root ; cloudflare reads it. | build fails on an old node | set the build variable (e.g. ). | blank page, console errors about a base path | don't set a vite unless serving from a subpath. | config change didn't apply | vars are build-time — redeploy after changing them. | self-hosting instead a (nginx, with spa fallback built in) is included if you'd rather run it yourself:

title: Introduction
description: Capsa is a self-hosted documentation platform — fast as in Vite, free as in libre.
excerpt: Capsa is a self-hosted documentation platform you own end to end. It renders MDX, ships a real command palette, an OpenAPI reference, theming, and AI-friendly exports — and deploys to Cloudflare Pages
body: capsa capsa is a self-hosted documentation platform you own end to end. it renders mdx, ships a real command palette, an openapi reference, theming, and ai-friendly exports — and deploys to cloudflare pages (or any static host) for free. a capsa was the cylindrical case a roman carried their scrolls in. this one holds yours. why capsa :::tip if you've used mintlify, capsa will feel familiar — but it's yours: no per-seat pricing, no vendor lock-in, and the whole thing is a vite + react app you can edit. ::: mdx content — write markdown, drop in react components when you need them. ⌘k command palette — instant, keyboard-first search over a build-time index. openapi reference — a full interactive api explorer (scalar) from your spec. theming — five built-in styles × light/dark, all token-driven and brandable. ai-native — generates , a per-page "copy as markdown", and "open in chatgpt/claude". authoring components — callouts, tabbed code samples, api method badges, "see also" cards. public by default — auth is opt-in; a fresh clone runs with no configuration. how it's organized markdown lives in . everything else is the platform — you rarely touch it. next <seealso> quickstart writing content deploy to cloudflare </seealso>

title: Navigation
description: Control the sidebar and top section tabs with a single declarative config.
excerpt: is the single source of truth for the sidebar and the top section tabs. It's declarative — edit the object, not the components.
body: navigation is the single source of truth for the sidebar and the top section tabs. it's declarative — edit the object, not the components. shape concepts tabs appear in the top bar. a tab either owns (its own sidebar) or is a single (like the api reference). groups are collapsible sidebar sections with an and optional (e.g. ). pages are doc slugs (the path under , no extension). list them explicitly for full control of order, or use to pull every page in a content folder. nesting — a page entry can itself be a group, for sub-sections. :::tip groups that resolve to zero pages (for example, filtered out by product scoping) are hidden automatically — no empty headers. ::: icons takes a short name mapped to a tabler icon in — e.g. , , , , , . add your own by extending that map. tags a renders a small accent badge next to the group label.

title: Writing Content
description: Frontmatter, Markdown, and the MDX components Capsa ships — callouts, code tabs, API badges, and more.
excerpt: Pages are : standard Markdown plus React components when you want them. This page is both the reference and a live demo of everything available.
body: writing content pages are : standard markdown plus react components when you want them. this page is both the reference and a live demo of everything available. frontmatter optional yaml at the top of a file controls metadata: callouts write , , , or . an optional overrides the label. :::note callouts use the theme's semantic tokens, so they re-color per theme and in light/dark automatically. ::: :::warning[heads up] wrap a label in brackets to set a custom title. ::: code blocks fenced code gets a language label, a copy button, and an optional filename via the meta: code tabs wrap multiple fenced blocks in ; the chosen language persists across every code-tab group on the site: <codetabs> </codetabs> api method references use inline to link prose to the api reference: <apimethod method="post" path="/v1/widgets" desc="create a widget" /> <apimethod method="get" path="/v1/widgets/{id}" desc="fetch a widget" /> see-also cards wraps a markdown link list in a discovery card: <seealso> configuration navigation </seealso> tables standard markdown tables scroll horizontally on small screens: component | use it for | -------------|-------------------------------------| | multi-language code samples | | notes, tips, warnings, dangers | | inline endpoint references | | related-page discovery cards |

title: Configuration
description: Environment variables, theming, the API reference, and analytics.
excerpt: Capsa is configured almost entirely through environment variables, so one codebase can power many deployments.
body: configuration capsa is configured almost entirely through environment variables, so one codebase can power many deployments. environment variables all are optional. a bare deploy needs none of them. variable | effect | ----------|--------| | brand shown in the top bar, titles, footer. defaults to . | | footer copyright name. defaults to . | | pin a theme ( , , , , ) and hide the switcher. | | base url — enables and absolute links in . | | scope nav + search to one product folder (multi-product setups). | / / | footer links — each renders only if set. | / | enable posthog analytics. off unless the key is set. | | opt into auth (see below). | | force public access even when auth is configured. | theming five styles ship in , each with light + dark: , , , , . users switch via the gear menu; pin one with to lock branding for a public site. colors are theme tokens — components never hardcode hex — so adding a brand theme is cloning a block in and registering it in . api reference drop an openapi spec at and it renders at (scalar). register versions in ; add a second and a version switcher appears automatically. authentication capsa is public by default. to gate the docs, set and wire to your provider — the included workos flow is a working example. with no client id, the auth providers never mount. generated artifacts every build writes to : (⌘k), (nav/seo), , + (ai agents), and per-page markdown under (the "copy page" action). all are git-ignored.

title: Quickstart
description: Clone, install, run, and publish your first page in a few minutes.
body: quickstart prerequisites node 18+ and pnpm ( ). create your docs use this repository as a github template (the use this template button), or clone it directly: run it that starts the dev server at . edit any file under and the page reloads. add a page create a markdown file anywhere under — the path becomes the route: then add it to the sidebar in : :::note pages don't have to be listed in to render — but only listed pages appear in the sidebar. use to include a whole folder. ::: build the build also generates the search index, sitemap, , and per-page markdown into . output lands in . publish <seealso> deploy to cloudflare pages configuration reference </seealso>

title: Theming
description: Use a built-in theme, pin one for your brand, or add your own.

headings.text: Pin a theme for your brand

excerpt: Capsa ships five theme styles, each with light and dark modes:
body: theming capsa ships five theme styles, each with light and dark modes: style | look | -------|------| | neutral, blue accent | | purple, dark-first | | silver/metallic, steel-blue accent | | warm, hard shadows, sharp corners | | slate, minimal (shadcn/ui palette) | users switch via the gear menu in the top bar (mode + style). pin a theme for your brand for a single-brand site, lock one style and hide the switcher: mode (light/dark/system) still works; only the style picker is hidden. how theming works colors are theme tokens, never hardcoded hex. components reference tokens like , , (resolved at runtime via tamagui), so switching themes recolors the whole ui — including code blocks, callouts, and the command palette. themes live in and are registered in . add a brand theme the fastest path is to clone an existing style and recolor it. 1. copy a preset block in — the block is a good neutral starting point. duplicate its palette and semantic-color objects, renaming → : 2. register it in the of the call: 3. add it to the type unions in ( , ) and the switch. 4. list it in the style switcher — in . 5. pin it with . :::tip start from the closest existing preset and change only the accent + neutral palettes. the 50 semantic tokens mostly cascade from those. ::: fonts the body/heading font is space grotesk ( ). swap it by changing that file and the import in .

title: Authentication
description: Capsa is public by default. Gate the docs behind a login when you need to.
excerpt: Capsa is public by default — a fresh clone runs with no auth providers mounted and every route open. Add auth only when your docs should be private.
body: authentication capsa is public by default — a fresh clone runs with no auth providers mounted and every route open. add auth only when your docs should be private. how it works derives two flags from the environment: no → auth providers never mount; renders through; and redirect straight to . client id set → the workos provider wraps the app, the login screen renders, and requires a session. → forces public access even when auth is configured (handy for local dev or a public mirror). enable the included workos flow capsa ships a working workos authkit example. 1. create a workos project and authkit client; copy the client id. 2. set the environment: 3. the flow is in and : workos signs the user in, the app exchanges the workos token with your backend, and the session is stored in a cookie. :::warning is a public client id — that's expected. any real secret (the token-exchange logic, api keys) must live in your backend, never in a variable. ::: use a different provider is the integration seam. to swap workos for auth0, clerk, your own sso, etc.: 1. replace the provider in (it's only mounted when ). 2. reimplement to expose the same shape — , , and a sign-in entry point. 3. and the routing keep working unchanged, since they only read those two flags. note on static hosting capsa is a static spa, so client-side auth gates the ui, not the bytes — the compiled docs are still downloadable by anyone who has the url. for truly private docs, put the site behind a network gate too (cloudflare access, a vpn, or an authenticating proxy) in addition to the in-app login.

title: API Reference
description: Render an interactive API explorer from your OpenAPI spec.
excerpt: Capsa renders a full interactive API reference from an OpenAPI document using Scalar. It lives at /docs/api and is lazy-loaded, so it stays out of the main bundle until visited.
body: api reference capsa renders a full interactive api reference from an openapi document using scalar. it lives at /docs/api and is lazy-loaded, so it stays out of the main bundle until visited. add your spec 1. drop your openapi document at (replacing the example spec). 2. that's it — visit . the api reference tab in the top bar links there; it's defined in : versions register each version in : with a single entry there's no switcher. add a second and a version dropdown appears on the reference page automatically. "try it" scalar's built-in api client is enabled — readers can execute requests with a key they paste (scalar stores it locally). to point it at a live sandbox, add a entry to your spec and enable cors for the docs origin on that server. customizing the reference everything below is configured in , in the object passed to . is reactive — it applies config changes in place via , so theme changes update without a remount (no flash). only key it on the spec , never on the theme. dark mode (wired for you) scalar keeps its own dark-mode state in , which drifts out of sync with the site. capsa forces it from the app theme: hiding chrome option | hides | --------|-------| | the developer tools / configure / share / deploy toolbar (capsa sets this) | | scalar's whole left sidebar | | scalar's search bar | | the schema/models section | | "download openapi spec" | | the "test request" buttons | | the api-client button | | switches from the default layout | and take — they default to showing on localhost, so set to hide them everywhere. theming (wired for you) scalar is a self-contained component and can't take tamagui props directly — it themes through its own css variables. capsa bridges them: it resolves your tamagui tokens with and feeds them in through , recomputed on every theme change: :::note this is a bridge, not real integration — you're copying resolved token values into scalar's variables, layered on top of scalar's default theme. map more variables (buttons, method colors, sidebar) to go further; anything you leave out keeps scalar's default. for full control instead, set and define every variable yourself. ::: keep the spec in sync if your spec is generated by an api repo, you don't have to copy it by hand. the included copies a spec into when is set — wire it into your build or a ci step: a common pattern: the api repo commits its spec to this repo (or triggers a rebuild) on change, so the reference is always current. inline references in prose use the component in any guide to link to an endpoint: <apimethod method="post" path="/v1/widgets" desc="create a widget" />
